summary: DHH argues that Apple has reached a tipping point of public scrutiny where its usual strategy of using legal and financial muscle to bully smaller opponents is no longer viable. He points to Apple dropping its DMCA lawsuit against Corellium as evidence that the company is running out of reputational capital even as its financial capital remains unlimited. He draws a parallel to Microsoft's post-antitrust transformation, suggesting that being forced to exercise restraint actually made Microsoft stronger. DHH concludes that Apple's newfound recognition of non-financial constraints on its power will ultimately benefit the company.
key_points: Apple has entered an era of default skepticism from media and the public, replacing years of unadulterated admiration
The dropped Corellium lawsuit signals Apple is learning that legal bullying carries reputational costs it can no longer afford
Financial capital alone cannot sustain fights on multiple fronts when political and reputational capital is depleted
Microsoft's post-antitrust restraint offers a model for how corporate humility can lead to long-term strength
A reminder of corporate mortality—that power has limits beyond money—is ultimately healthy for a company
money_quotes: Apple can lay legal siege for longer than most opponents can stay solvent. Them be the spoils of a trillion-dollar market cap.
The merits of a case are only of secondary concern to whether the target can afford to fight the siege. Most can't.
They'll run out of critical good-will with groups that matter far sooner than they'll run the treasury dry.
Apple has been taking on too many fights on too many fronts.
A reminder of your own corporate mortality is good for business. You can't fight all the world all the time.
Ironically, I think the prudence Apple is discovering – now they're realizing that there are other constraints on their power than just money – might well make the company stronger long term.
key_insight: Companies with unlimited financial capital still face hard limits on their reputational and political capital, and learning to respect those limits ultimately makes them stronger.
